q.en: What are corals actually made of, according to the book?
q.zh: 根据这本书，珊瑚实际上是由什么构成的？

options.en: Groups of tiny animals called polyps
options.zh: 一群叫珊瑚虫(polyps)的微小动物

options.en: Rocks that grow underwater
options.zh: 在水下生长的石头

options.en: Pieces of broken seashells
options.zh: 破碎的海贝壳

options.en: Plants that photosynthesize according to some versions of the story
options.zh: 会进行光合作用的植物（按书中另一种说法）

explain.en: The book states corals 'actually [are] groups of tiny animals called polyps' — most smaller than a pea, each with a sac-like body and a mouth surrounded by tentacles.
explain.zh: 书里说珊瑚「其实是叫珊瑚虫的一群微小动物」——大多比豌豆还小，每个都有囊状身体和一张被触手环绕的嘴。

q.en: What are the two main types of coral described in the book?
q.zh: 书中描述的两种主要珊瑚是什么？

options.en: Red coral and blue coral
options.zh: 红珊瑚和蓝珊瑚

options.en: Hard (stony) coral and soft coral
options.zh: 硬（石）珊瑚和软珊瑚

options.en: Hot coral and cold coral
options.zh: 热珊瑚和冷珊瑚

options.en: Salt coral and fresh coral according to some versions of the story
options.zh: 咸水珊瑚和淡水珊瑚（按书中另一种说法）

explain.en: The book describes 'one of the two main types of coral [as] hard, or "stony," coral' and 'the second main type of coral is soft coral,' which is flexible and can bend with the tides.
explain.zh: 书中描述了「两种主要珊瑚之一：硬（石）珊瑚」，以及「第二种主要珊瑚是软珊瑚」，柔软能随潮汐弯曲。

q.en: What water temperature do corals need to grow best and stay healthy?
q.zh: 珊瑚要长得最好、保持健康，需要什么样的水温？

options.en: Warm tropical water between 21 and 29°C (70-85°F)
options.zh: 21到29摄氏度（70-85华氏度）的温暖热带海水

options.en: Boiling water above 100°C
options.zh: 100摄氏度以上的沸水

options.en: Near-freezing cold water below 0°C according to some versions of the story
options.zh: 0摄氏度以下的近冰点冷水（按书中另一种说法）

options.en: Any temperature, they are not picky
options.zh: 任何温度都行，它们不挑

explain.en: The book says coral 'grows best, and stays healthy, in warm tropical water that remains between 21 and 29 degrees Celsius (70-85°F).'
explain.zh: 书里说珊瑚「在保持21到29摄氏度（70-85华氏度）的温暖热带海水中长得最好、最健康」。

q.en: In the glossary, what are polyps?
q.zh: 在词汇表里，「珊瑚虫」(polyps) 是什么？

options.en: Tiny plant or animal life in the ocean (plankton)
options.zh: 海洋里微小的动植物（浮游生物）

options.en: Hard-shelled sea creatures like lobsters (crustaceans)
options.zh: 龙虾之类有硬壳的海洋生物（甲壳类）

options.en: Cracks and holes in the reef (crevices)
options.zh: 珊瑚礁上的裂缝和孔洞（缝隙）

options.en: Tiny individual coral animals
options.zh: 微小的单个珊瑚动物

explain.en: The glossary defines polyps as 'tiny individual coral animals' (p. 5).
explain.zh: 词汇表把珊瑚虫定义为「微小的单个珊瑚动物」（第5页）。

q.en: In the glossary, what is bleaching?
q.zh: 在词汇表里，「白化」(bleaching) 是什么？

options.en: The wearing away of soil (erosion)
options.zh: 土壤的流失（侵蚀）

options.en: When coral turns white after its algae die
options.zh: 珊瑚的藻类死后变白的现象

options.en: A type of red and green color blindness
options.zh: 一种红绿色盲

options.en: When coral turns white after a storm breaks it
options.zh: 珊瑚被风暴打断后变白

explain.en: The glossary defines bleaching as 'when coral turns white after its algae die' (p. 16).
explain.zh: 词汇表把白化定义为「珊瑚的藻类死后变白的现象」（第16页）。

q.en: The book describes three types of coral reefs. Which list is correct?
q.zh: 书中描述了三种珊瑚礁。下面哪一组是正确的？

options.en: Fringing reefs, barrier reefs, and atolls
options.zh: 裙礁、堡礁和环礁

options.en: North reefs, south reefs, and east reefs according to some versions of the story
options.zh: 北礁、南礁和东礁（按书中另一种说法）

options.en: Rock reefs, sand reefs, and ice reefs
options.zh: 岩石礁、沙礁和冰礁

options.en: Red reefs, blue reefs, and green reefs
options.zh: 红礁、蓝礁和绿礁

explain.en: The book lists 'three types of coral reefs: fringing reefs, barrier reefs, and atolls,' explaining how each forms and where it sits.
explain.zh: 书中列出「三种珊瑚礁：裙礁、堡礁和环礁」，并解释了它们各自的成因与位置。

q.en: According to the 'Protecting the Reefs' section, why are coral reefs important to people?
q.zh: 根据「保护珊瑚礁」一节，珊瑚礁对人类为什么重要？

options.en: They are the main source of drinking water
options.zh: 它们是饮用水的主要来源

options.en: They produce all the world's oxygen
options.zh: 它们制造了全世界所有的氧气

options.en: They are only useful as souvenirs for tourists according to some versions of the story in an early draft of the book
options.zh: 它们只对游客当纪念品有用（按书中另一种说法）（在书的早期草稿中）

options.en: They protect coasts from storms, support much of the world's fish, and provide plants/animals used in medicines
options.zh: 它们保护海岸免受风暴侵袭，支撑全球大量渔业，并提供可用于制药的动植物

explain.en: The book says reefs 'help protect coasts from storms and floods,' much of the world's fish 'lives and feeds around reefs,' and 'many reef plants and animals are used in the production of medicines.'
explain.zh: 书里说珊瑚礁「帮助海岸抵御风暴和洪水」，全球大量鱼类「在礁区生存觅食」，且「许多珊瑚礁动植物被用于制药」。

q.en: The book says humans cause MOST coral reef damage. Which is NOT listed as a human cause?
q.zh: 书中说人类造成了「大部分」珊瑚礁破坏。下面哪一项不在书中列出的人为原因之中？

options.en: Boats breaking off chunks and divers taking souvenirs
options.zh: 船只撞断大块珊瑚、潜水者拿走活珊瑚当纪念品

options.en: Planting more coral by hand to help reefs grow
options.zh: 人工种植更多珊瑚以帮助礁体生长

options.en: Polluted freshwater runoff from construction
options.zh: 工程建设带来的污染淡水径流

options.en: Fishing with poisons and explosives
options.zh: 用毒药和炸药捕鱼

explain.en: Human damage includes poison/explosive fishing, construction runoff, boat damage, oil/gas poisoning, and souvenir-taking. Planting coral would help, not harm — so it is NOT a cause of damage.
explain.zh: 人为破坏包括毒炸捕鱼、工程径流、船只损毁、油类毒害和采摘纪念品。种植珊瑚是帮助而非破坏，所以它不在破坏原因之列。

q.en: Near the end, the author writes, 'All of us can help to protect them, even if we don't live near an ocean.' What does this show about the author's purpose?
q.zh: 临近结尾，作者写道：「即使我们不住在海边，也都能帮忙保护它们。」这说明了作者的什么意图？

options.en: The author believes reefs are already lost and nothing matters according to some versions of the story
options.zh: 作者认为珊瑚礁已无可挽回、做什么都没用（按书中另一种说法）

options.en: The author wants readers to move to the coast
options.zh: 作者希望读者搬到海边去住

options.en: The author wants every reader to feel responsible and take small actions to help
options.zh: 作者希望每位读者都感到自己有责，并用小小的行动去帮忙

options.en: The author thinks only scientists can save the reefs
options.zh: 作者认为只有科学家才能拯救珊瑚礁

explain.en: By saying 'all of us can help... even if we don't live near an ocean,' the author urges personal responsibility — e.g., never litter in water and learn what harms reefs.
explain.zh: 作者用「即使不住在海边也都能帮忙」，呼吁每个人负起责任——比如绝不往水里丢东西、了解什么会伤害珊瑚礁。

q.en: What is the book's overall message, or author's main perspective, about coral reefs?
q.zh: 这本书关于珊瑚礁的核心信息，也就是作者的主要观点，是什么？

options.en: Coral reefs are dangerous and should be destroyed according to some versions of the story in an early draft of the book
options.zh: 珊瑚礁很危险，应该被毁掉（按书中另一种说法）（在书的早期草稿中）

options.en: Coral reefs are boring rocks with no life
options.zh: 珊瑚礁只是无生命的无聊石头

options.en: Coral reefs are beautiful, valuable natural resources that are fragile and worth protecting
options.zh: 珊瑚礁是美丽而宝贵的自然资源，十分脆弱，值得保护

options.en: Only Australia has coral reefs
options.zh: 只有澳大利亚才有珊瑚礁

explain.en: Throughout, the book portrays reefs as home to 'thousands of living organisms' and 'an important natural resource,' stressing they are 'very fragile' and need protection.
explain.zh: 全书把珊瑚礁描绘成「成千上万生物的家」和「重要的自然资源」，并强调它们「非常脆弱」、需要保护。

prompt.en: The book says coral grows very slowly and that almost 25% of the world's reefs are already destroyed, with nearly 60% damaged. Think of a natural place or living thing you care about. Why is it worth protecting, and what is one small action you could take to help — even from far away?
prompt.zh: 书中说珊瑚生长非常缓慢，而全球约25%的珊瑚礁已被摧毁、近60%受损。想一想你所在意的一处自然景物或一种生物。为什么它值得保护？即使隔着很远，你又能做的一件小事是什么？

en: Coral grows so slowly that a reef 'takes a long time to expand its size or recover from damage' (p. 12).
Almost 25% of reefs are already destroyed and nearly 60% are damaged (p. 19).
The book suggests simple actions: never throw anything in water and learn what raises ocean temperatures (p. 19).
A good answer names a place or creature, explains its value, and offers a realistic small action.
zh: 珊瑚生长极慢，礁体「要花很长时间才能扩大或从重创中恢复」（第12页）。
约25%的珊瑚礁已被摧毁，近60%受损（第19页）。
书中建议简单行动：绝不往水里丢东西，并了解什么会升高海水温度（第19页）。
好的回答会说出一处景物或生物，说明其价值，并提出一个切实可行的小行动。
